case_name: Rivera v. New York City Health & Hosps. Corp.

summary.brief: Rivera v. New York City Health & Hosps. Corp.. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff treated at Woodhull as her primary health care provider for various ailments since June 2003.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 16, 2014, she was treated at Woodhull's emergency department with shortness of breath and numbness in her left arm and shoulder.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 14, 2016, Plaintiff saw Dr. Zaki for prescription refills and care for her diabetes and hepatitis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff alleges that HHC/Woodhull departed from good and accepted standards of medical practice by failing to properly diagnose her cervical rib anomaly.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sherman opines that a cervical rib is 'difficult to visualize on many types of radiological studies' and it is especially difficult to visualize on an MRI of the cervical spine.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Sherman

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff's radiology expert counters Dr. Sherman's expert opinion that a cervical rib is typically asymptomatic and therefore not a pertinent detail.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's radiology expert

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff's vascular surgery expert opines that these omissions from the CT scan report were a proximate cause of Plaintiff's alleged injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's vascular surgery expert

evidence_index.text: ORDERED that Defendant New York City Health & Hospital Corp.'s motion (Seq. No. 3) for summary judgment is GRANTED TO THE EXTENT of dismissing Plaintiff's medical malpractice claims except those claims related to the radiological review and report of the September 8, 2015 CT scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court order

evidence_index.text: The conflicting expert opinions on whether HHC's radiologist deviated from community standards by not including the cervical rib and post-stenotic dilatation findings, and whether that deviation resulted in a failure to properly diagnose and treat Plaintiff's condition in the two years she treated at Woodhull before her emergency thrombectomy on August 28, 2017, raise issues of fact and credibility which preclude summary judgment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient has a history of neck and shoulder pain, with intermittent symptoms including numbness and burning in the arms. She was treated for various conditions including diabetes and hypertension.

focused_exam.neck: Tenderness noted, lump found on right side
focused_exam.upper_extremities: Burning sensation in right arm and hand

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Neck and shoulder pain with possible nerve involvement

requestables.name: CT Scan with Contrast
requestables.description: CT scan of neck to evaluate lump and pain

reveal.result_summary: Cervical rib visible, post-stenotic dilatation of subclavian artery
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt referral to vascular surgery

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with neck and shoulder pain

key_events.event: Patient reports neck pain and lump in neck

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: CT scan performed to evaluate neck lump

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with neck pain and lump in neck, CT scan performed
decision_points.prompt: What should be done after reviewing the CT scan?

options.description: Refer to vascular surgery for evaluation of cervical rib and artery involvement
options.clinical_reasoning: Presence of cervical rib and artery involvement requires specialist evaluation

actual_action_defendant.description: No further action taken, treated symptomatically

expected_action_court.description: Referral to vascular surgery should have been made
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for specialist evaluation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to recognize the clinical significance of the cervical rib and artery involvement
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral to vascular surgery for further evaluation and management
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed thrombosis in right subclavian artery requiring emergency surgery
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Persisting numbness and disability in right arm

key_findings: Failure to diagnose cervical rib and artery involvement from CT scan
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented complications

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to act on CT scan findings
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Referral to vascular surgery could have prevented thrombosis

factual_timeline.event: CT scan performed, cervical rib visible
Patient treated in Cuba for thrombosis

tests_performed.result: Cervical rib and post-stenotic dilatation visible

diagnoses.diagnosis: Cervical rib with subclavian artery involvement
diagnoses.when_made: Not diagnosed until after emergency treatment

treatments.outcome: Blood clot cleared, blood flow reestablished

allegations.allegation: Failure to diagnose cervical rib and artery involvement

court_findings.finding: Radiologist failed to report cervical rib and artery involvement
